docs.title: dygraphs
docs.text:
Python wrapper for the dygraphs JavaScript charting library.

Core port of the R dygraphs package — all 37 exported R dy* functions plus the dygraph() constructor are ported to a Pythonic builder API. The R package (by RStudio/JJ Allaire) is the most mature dygraphs wrapper in any language; this package faithfully ports its API design, data model, and test coverage to Python. See the R Function Mapping table below.

Framework-agnostic core with adapters for Plotly Dash and Shiny for Python.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Two API styles: builder chaining (Dygraph(df).options(...).series(...)) and declarative (Dygraph(df, options=Options(...), series=[Series(...)])) — both produce identical output
  • Full R port + full JS coverage: options, axes, series, legend, highlight, annotations, shadings, events, limits, range selector, roller, callbacks — every documented and undocumented dygraph option is exposed
  • Advanced plotters: bar chart, stacked bar, candlestick, multi-column, stem, filled line, error fill + group variants
  • Plugins: unzoom, crosshair, ribbon, rebase
  • Error bars: symmetric, custom (low/mid/high), fractions with Wilson intervals
  • Zoom sync across multiple charts with debounced range-selector panning
  • Modebar overlay (Plotly-style) with PNG download and reset zoom buttons
  • Standalone HTML export via .to_html() — no server needed
  • dash-capture compatible via dygraph_strategy()
  • Framework-agnostic: core builder has zero Dash/Shiny imports
  • Flexible data input: DataFrame, Series, dict, list, numpy array, CSV string, CSV file
  • Type-safe: full type hints, py.typed, passes ty and ruff

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install dygraphs            # core only
pip install dygraphs[dash]      # + Plotly Dash adapter
pip install dygraphs[shiny]     # + Shiny for Python adapter

docs.title: Mental model
docs.text:
One builder, four rendering paths. The builder stays framework-agnostic; each framework wraps it with a separate, small adapter — same split as plotly's Figure and R's htmlwidget.

                   ┌──────────────────────────┐
                   │  Dygraph(df, ...)        │
                   │  .options(...)           │
                   │  .series(...)            │   ← builder, framework-agnostic
                   │  .range_selector(...)    │     (pure Python data class)
                   └────────────┬─────────────┘
                                │
         ┌──────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┐
         │                      │                          │
         ▼                      ▼                          ▼
  DygraphChart(           dygraph_ui(id)            dg.to_html()
    figure=dg,            render_dygraph(             → standalone
    id="chart")             session, id, dg)            HTML string
    → Dash                → Shiny                    → no server
  • Dash — build layout with DygraphChart(figure=dg, id="chart"); update via Output(chart, "data") returning Dygraph(...).to_js().
  • Shiny — dygraph_ui("chart") in the UI, render_dygraph(session, "chart", dg) in the server.
  • Standalone HTML — dg.to_html() returns a complete, self-contained HTML page (for reports, emails, iframes).
  • Introspection / JSON — dg.to_dict() (raw, with JS() objects) or dg.to_js() (JSON-safe, string markers for JS code).

docs.title: Recipes
docs.text:
Short answers to the most common questions. Deeper sections below.

I want to build a chart and view it in a notebook.

chart = Dygraph(df, title="Demo").range_selector()
chart  # auto-displays via _repr_html_

I want to render it in Dash.

from dygraphs.dash import DygraphChart
app.layout = html.Div([DygraphChart(figure=chart, id="my-chart")])

I want to update the chart from a Dash callback.

@callback(Output(chart, "data"), Input("btn", "n_clicks"))
def refresh(n):
    return Dygraph(df * n).to_js()   # .to_js() is the Dash-safe serialiser

I want an empty placeholder that a callback fills in later.

chart = DygraphChart(None, id="chart")   # data=None; renderer is a no-op

I want to read the chart's id from Python.

chart.chart_id   # "my-chart" — NOT chart.id (Dash reserves .id for its
                 #             duplicate-id validator, so we use a
                 #             different attribute name)

I want symmetric error bars.

data = Dygraph.error_bar_data(x=dates, y=values, error=stddev)
chart = Dygraph(data, options={"error_bars": True})

I want to sync zoom across multiple charts.

Dygraph(df1, group="sync")
Dygraph(df2, group="sync")     # same group name → shared x-axis window

I want to export a chart to a single HTML file.

Path("chart.html").write_text(Dygraph(df).to_html())

docs.title: Builder API (chaining)
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
from dygraphs import Dygraph

df = pd.DataFrame(
    {"temp": [10, 12, 11, 14, 13], "rain": [5, 3, 7, 2, 6]},
    index=pd.date_range("2024-01-01", periods=5, freq="D"),
)

chart = (
    Dygraph(df, title="Weather")
    .options(fill_graph=True, draw_points=True, colors=["#00d4aa", "#f4a261"])
    .axis("y", label="Value")
    .series("temp", stroke_width=2)
    .legend(show="always")
    .range_selector(height=30)
)

docs.title: Declarative API
docs.text:
from dygraphs import Dygraph, Options, Series, Axis, Legend, RangeSelector

chart = Dygraph(
    df,
    title="Weather",
    options=Options(fill_graph=True, draw_points=True, colors=["#00d4aa", "#f4a261"]),
    axes=[Axis("y", label="Value")],
    series=[Series("temp", stroke_width=2)],
    legend=Legend(show="always"),
    range_selector=RangeSelector(height=30),
)

Dicts work too — mix freely:

chart = Dygraph(
    df,
    title="Weather",
    options={"fill_graph": True},
    series=[Series("temp", color="red"), {"name": "rain", "axis": "y2"}],
)

docs.title: Render in Dash
docs.text:
A Dygraph is framework-agnostic (same pattern as plotly.graph_objects.Figure). For Dash, wrap it with DygraphChart:

from dash import Dash, html
from dygraphs.dash import DygraphChart

app = Dash(__name__)
app.layout = html.Div([DygraphChart(figure=chart, id="my-chart")])

docs.title: Render in Shiny
docs.text:
from dygraphs.shiny import dygraph_ui, render_dygraph

# In UI:
dygraph_ui("my-chart")

# In server:
await render_dygraph(session, "my-chart", chart)

docs.title: Standalone HTML
docs.text:
from pathlib import Path

html_string = chart.to_html()
Path("chart.html").write_text(html_string)

docs.title: Jupyter / IPython
docs.text:
A Dygraph is its own display object — making it the last expression in a cell auto-renders it inline via _repr_html_, the same UX as dygraph() in RStudio's viewer:

chart  # auto-displays

For loops or non-last-expression contexts, use the explicit helper:

chart.show()  # uses IPython.display.HTML

docs.title: Data Input
docs.text:
Format Example pandas DataFrame pd.DataFrame({"y": [1,2,3]}, index=pd.date_range(...)) pandas Series pd.Series([1,2,3], name="y") dict of lists {"x": [1,2,3], "y": [10,20,30]} list of rows [[1, 10], [2, 20], [3, 30]] numpy array np.array([[1, 10], [2, 20]]) CSV string "Date,A,B\n2024-01-01,1,2\n..." CSV file Dygraph.from_csv("data.csv")

DatetimeIndex is auto-detected and formatted for the x-axis.

docs.title: Builder Methods
docs.text:
All configuration uses method chaining, mirroring the R package's pipe operator:

chart = (
    Dygraph(df, title="My Chart")
    .options(fill_graph=True, stroke_width=2)
    .axis("y", label="Temperature", value_range=(0, 40))
    .axis("y2", label="Humidity", independent_ticks=True)
    .series("temp", color="red", axis="y")
    .series("humidity", color="blue", axis="y2")
    .legend(show="always")
    .highlight(circle_size=5, series_opts={"strokeWidth": 3})
    .annotation("2024-03-15", "A", tooltip="Spring")
    .shading("2024-01-15", "2024-02-15", color="rgba(200,200,255,0.3)")
    .event("2024-02-14", "Valentine's", color="#f76e8a")
    .limit(30.0, "Threshold", stroke_pattern="dotted")
    .range_selector(height=30)
    .roller(roll_period=7)
    .crosshair(direction="vertical")
    .unzoom()
)
dash_component = DygraphChart(figure=chart, id="my-chart")
Method R Equivalent Description Dygraph(data, ...) dygraph() Create chart (also accepts periodicity=) .options(...) dyOptions() Global options .axis(name, ...) dyAxis() Per-axis config .series(name, ...) dySeries() Per-series config (accepts [names] for error bands) .group(names, ...) dyGroup() Group of series — shared display options .sync_group(name) — Cross-chart sync alias for group= kwarg .legend(...) dyLegend() Legend options .highlight(...) dyHighlight() Highlight behavior .annotation(x, text) dyAnnotation() Data annotations .shading(from_, to) dyShading() Background regions .event(x, label) dyEvent() Vertical event lines .limit(value, label) dyLimit() Horizontal limit lines .range_selector(...) dyRangeSelector() Range selector .roller(...) dyRoller() Rolling average .callbacks(...) dyCallbacks() JS callbacks .css(css) dyCSS() Custom CSS — file path or raw CSS string .dependency(name, ...) dyDependency() Attach external JS / CSS files

docs.title: Plotters
docs.text:
.bar_chart(), .stacked_bar_chart(), .multi_column(), .candlestick(), .bar_series(name), .stem_series(name), .shadow(name), .filled_line(name), .error_fill(name) + group variants

Also via constructor: Dygraph(df, plotter="bar_chart")

docs.title: Plugins
docs.text: .unzoom(), .crosshair(direction), .ribbon(data, palette), .rebase(value, percent)

docs.title: Error Bars
docs.text:
from dygraphs import Dygraph

data = Dygraph.error_bar_data(x=[1, 2, 3], y=[10, 20, 30], error=[1, 2, 3])
chart = Dygraph(data, options={"error_bars": True})

docs.title: Syncing Multiple Charts
docs.text:
Charts with the same group name automatically sync zoom, pan, and highlight:

from dygraphs import Dygraph
from dygraphs.dash import DygraphChart, StackedBarChart

chart_a = DygraphChart(figure=Dygraph(df1, group="sync").range_selector(), id="a")
chart_b = DygraphChart(figure=Dygraph(df2, group="sync").range_selector(), id="b")
chart_c = StackedBarChart(id="c", initial_data=csv_data, title="Stacked Bar", group="sync")

app.layout = html.Div([chart_a, chart_b, chart_c])

docs.title: Updating data from Dash callbacks
docs.text: dygraphs charts in Dash follow the R htmlwidgets model: every config update destroys the existing dygraph instance and creates a new one from scratch. There is exactly one update path, so there's no class of "did I forget to invalidate X?" bugs to chase.

docs.title: What a DygraphChart actually is
docs.text:
The abstraction is deliberately thin and worth stating honestly — no magic outside what dash-wrap documents.

A DygraphChart is a plain html.Div containing two sibling components:

Component Purpose dcc.Store(id={id}) Chart store — canonical config; the chart's identity. html.Div(id={id}-container) Container where the dygraphs JS renders its DOM.

DygraphChart subclasses dash_wrap.ComponentWrapper with the store as the inner component. Two small pieces of machinery make Output(chart, "data") resolve to the store:

  • _set_random_id is overridden on the wrapper to return the inner store's id. Dash's dependency wiring calls this when it sees a component (not a string) passed to Output / Input / State.
  • __class__ is a property that returns the inner store's class, so isinstance(chart, dcc.Store) is True. The C-level type(chart) is still DygraphChart, which is what Dash's serialiser uses to emit the DOM (a normal <div>).

A per-instance clientside callback, registered at construction, listens to the store and calls window.dygraphsDash.render(setup, config). The renderer JS itself lives in src/dygraphs/assets/dash_render.js (real .js file, lintable).

docs.title: Writing callbacks
docs.text:
One write surface: the store. Every change — data, attrs, styling — flows through it. Both the component-reference form and the string-id form work; they're equivalent to Dash's dependency wiring.

import dash
from dash import Input, Output
from dygraphs import Dygraph
from dygraphs.dash import DygraphChart

chart = DygraphChart(figure=Dygraph(df), id="my-chart")

# Output(chart, "data") and Output("my-chart", "data") are equivalent.
# ``to_js()`` is the Dash-callback-safe counterpart of ``to_dict()``:
# embedded ``JS(code)`` objects (default interaction model, custom
# plotters, user callbacks) are replaced by ``"__JS__:code:__JS__"``
# string markers so Dash's prop validator accepts them; the clientside
# renderer evaluates the markers back to real JS at render time.
@dash.callback(Output(chart, "data"), Input("refresh", "n_clicks"))
def refresh(_n):
    return Dygraph(new_df).to_js()

# Styling changes go through the same channel — build a fresh config
# with the new options.  Destroy+recreate is cheap for typical charts.
@dash.callback(Output(chart, "data"), Input("toggle", "value"))
def toggle(v):
    return Dygraph(df).options(stroke_width=3 if v else 1).to_js()

If you hit a performance wall with large datasets + frequent cosmetic updates, see CLAUDE.md → Decisions deferred / opts store for the preserved design of a cheaper override channel.

docs.title: Empty placeholder
docs.text:
Sometimes the first real config arrives only after a callback fires (e.g. a button click, a URL-query parse). Pass figure=None to build an empty DygraphChart whose store holds data=None:

chart = DygraphChart(None, id="my-chart")

The clientside renderer has an existing if (!config) return; guard, so the chart is a no-op until real data is pushed via Output(chart, "data"). Callbacks wire up normally — the chart is a real component with a real id from the moment the layout is built.

docs.title: Preserving zoom across updates
docs.text:
Because every config update is a full destroy+recreate, the user's current zoom range is discarded by default — same as R's dygraph(...) with retainDateWindow = FALSE. To carry the zoom forward across updates, set retain_date_window=True on the chart options:

chart = (
    Dygraph(df, title="Live data")
    .options(retain_date_window=True)
    .range_selector(height=30)
)
dash_component = DygraphChart(figure=chart, id="my-chart")

The renderer reads the previous instance's xAxisRange() before destroying it and writes it back into the new instance's dateWindow.

docs.title: Group sync still works
docs.text: Charts that share a group= name continue to sync zoom, pan, and highlight across updates — the JS group registry is keyed by chart id, not by dygraph instance, so a destroy+recreate transparently re-registers under the same id.

docs.title: Modebar
docs.text:
Plotly-style overlay buttons appear on hover:

  • Camera icon: Download chart as PNG (hides range selector)
  • Home icon: Reset zoom to full range

Disable with DygraphChart(..., modebar=False).

docs.title: dash-capture Integration
docs.text:
from dygraphs.dash import dygraph_strategy
from dash_capture import capture_element

capture_element(
    app, "btn", "chart-container", "img-store",
    strategy=dygraph_strategy(hide_range_selector=True),
)

docs.title: Utility Methods
docs.text:
chart.update(legend={"show": "follow"})  # modify config after construction
forked = chart.copy()                     # deep copy for variants
config = chart.to_dict()                  # plain dict (framework-agnostic)
json_str = chart.to_json()                # JSON with JS functions

docs.title: Core
docs.text:
Builder for a dygraphs chart — framework-agnostic config object.

Port of the R dygraph() constructor and the full family of dy*() pipe functions (dyOptions, dySeries, dyAxis …), expressed as chained method calls. Produces a plain dict via :meth:to_dict / :meth:to_js, standalone HTML via :meth:to_html, or plugs into :class:dygraphs.dash.DygraphChart (Dash) and :func:dygraphs.shiny.dygraph_ui / :func:~dygraphs.shiny.render_dygraph (Shiny) for interactive rendering.

Two equivalent construction styles are supported and can be mixed:

  • Builder (chained methods) — mirrors R's pipe idiom, the most common style. All configuration happens via methods like :meth:options, :meth:series, :meth:axis, :meth:legend after construction.
  • Declarative (keyword dataclasses) — the same configuration can be passed to Dygraph(...) directly via the declarative parameters below. Each accepts either a dataclass instance (Options(fill_graph=True)) or a plain dict with the same keys ({"fill_graph": True}). Useful when loading config from YAML / JSON, or when users prefer a single constructor call over method chaining.
PARAMETER DESCRIPTION data

Chart data. Accepted shapes:

  • pd.DataFrame — index is the x-axis (DatetimeIndex → date axis, otherwise numeric); columns are the series.
  • pd.Series — promoted to a single-column DataFrame.
  • dict — first key is x-axis, remaining keys are series.
  • list[list] — row-oriented; first column is x-axis.
  • np.ndarray — 1-D treated as a single series, 2-D as rows.
  • str — parsed as CSV. First column becomes the x-axis.

Mixed timezone / naive datetime indexes are normalised to ISO strings on output.

TYPE: pd.DataFrame | pd.Series | dict[str, list] | list[list] | np.ndarray | str

title

Chart title (main in R). Rendered above the plot. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

xlab

X-axis label. By default None (inferred from the data: index name if named, else "Date" for date axes / "x" otherwise).

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

ylab

Y-axis label. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

periodicity

Manually override the auto-detected periodicity of date data. Only valid for date-formatted data — passing it with numeric data raises :class:ValueError. Mirrors R's dygraph(..., periodicity=...). By default None, in which case the scale is inferred from the pandas index spacing.

TYPE: ('yearly', 'quarterly', 'monthly', 'weekly', 'daily', 'hourly', 'minute', 'seconds', 'milliseconds') DEFAULT: "yearly"

group

Cross-chart synchronisation group name. Charts that share the same name auto-sync their x-axis zoom, pan, and highlight. Mirrors R's dygraph(group=). Equivalent to the chainable :meth:sync_group alias. Not the same as the :meth:group builder method, which takes a list of column names and mirrors R's dyGroup() to style a set of series together — the name collision is intentional, both mirror the R API verbatim. By default None (no group — chart is standalone).

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

width

Chart width in pixels. By default None (auto — fills container).

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

height

Chart height in pixels. By default None (auto).

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION options

Global dygraph options (dyOptions in R). Field names match the builder method :meth:options — e.g. Options(fill_graph=True, stroke_width=2) or {"fill_graph": True, "stroke_width": 2}. Sets things like stacking, fill, stroke style, colour palette, rolling period, etc. Full field list: :class:dygraphs.declarative.Options. By default None.

TYPE: :class:`~dygraphs.Options` | dict | None

axes

Per-axis configuration (dyAxis in R). Accepts a dict keyed by axis name ("x", "y", "y2") or a list of :class:~dygraphs.Axis / dicts (each must include name). Configures label text, value range, tick formatting, log scale, grid, etc. Full field list: :class:dygraphs.declarative.Axis. By default None.

TYPE: dict[str, :class:`~dygraphs.Axis`] | list[:class:`~dygraphs.Axis`] | None

series

Per-series display options (dySeries in R). Each entry configures one series by name — colour, stroke width, axis assignment, step plot, etc. Full field list: :class:dygraphs.declarative.Series. By default None.

TYPE: list[:class:`~dygraphs.Series` | dict] | None

legend

Legend configuration (dyLegend in R): show mode ("auto" / "always" / "follow" / "never"), width, label layout, follow-cursor offsets. Full field list: :class:dygraphs.declarative.Legend. By default None.

TYPE: :class:`~dygraphs.Legend` | dict | None

highlight

Hover-highlight behaviour (dyHighlight in R): circle size, highlight-series-opts, hide-on-mouseout. By default None.

TYPE: :class:`~dygraphs.Highlight` | dict | None

annotations

List of annotations (dyAnnotation in R). Each points at an x-value with short text, tooltip, colour, and optional click handlers. By default None.

TYPE: list[:class:`~dygraphs.Annotation` | dict] | None

shadings

List of shaded regions (dyShading in R). Each has from_ and to bounds on the x- or y-axis, plus a fill colour. By default None.

TYPE: list[:class:`~dygraphs.Shading` | dict] | None

events

List of vertical event lines (dyEvent in R). Each pins a labelled line at an x-value. By default None.

TYPE: list[:class:`~dygraphs.Event` | dict] | None

limits

List of horizontal limit lines (dyLimit in R). By default None.

TYPE: list[:class:`~dygraphs.Limit` | dict] | None

range_selector

Range-selector configuration (dyRangeSelector in R): height, initial date_window, fill/stroke colours, keep_mouse_zoom, retain_date_window. See :class:dygraphs.declarative.RangeSelector. By default None (no range selector).

TYPE: :class:`~dygraphs.RangeSelector` | dict | None

roller

Rolling-average widget (dyRoller in R): show widget, initial roll period. By default None.

TYPE: :class:`~dygraphs.Roller` | dict | None

callbacks

JavaScript event callbacks (dyCallbacks in R): click, zoom, highlight, draw, point-click handlers. Values are raw JS source strings (wrapped in :class:~dygraphs.JS for type clarity, but plain strings also work). By default None.

TYPE: :class:`~dygraphs.Callbacks` | dict | None

plotter

Chart-level plotter shortcut — one of "bar_chart", "stacked_bar_chart", "multi_column", "candlestick". Equivalent to calling the matching builder method (e.g. :meth:bar_chart) after construction. By default None (default line plotter).

TYPE: str | None

RAISES DESCRIPTION ValueError

If periodicity is given for numeric (non-date) data, if periodicity is not one of the accepted values, or if data has an unsupported shape (e.g. empty CSV, < 2 columns).

Examples:

From a pandas DataFrame (builder style):

>>> import pandas as pd
>>> df = pd.DataFrame(
...     {"temp": [10, 12, 11], "rain": [5, 3, 7]},
...     index=pd.date_range("2024-01-01", periods=3),
... )
>>> chart = (
...     Dygraph(df, title="Weather")
...     .options(fill_graph=True, stroke_width=2)
...     .series("temp", color="red")
...     .axis("y", label="°C")
...     .range_selector(height=30)
... )

The same chart in declarative form:

>>> from dygraphs import Options, Series, Axis, RangeSelector
>>> chart = Dygraph(
...     df,
...     title="Weather",
...     options=Options(fill_graph=True, stroke_width=2),
...     series=[Series("temp", color="red")],
...     axes={"y": Axis(name="y", label="°C")},
...     range_selector=RangeSelector(height=30),
... )

Declarative accepts plain dicts too — mix freely:

>>> chart = Dygraph(
...     df,
...     options={"fill_graph": True},
...     series=[Series("temp", color="red"), {"name": "rain", "axis": "y2"}],
... )

From a CSV string:

>>> chart = Dygraph("Date,A,B\n2024-01-01,1,2\n2024-01-02,3,4")

Synchronise two charts on the x-axis:

>>> a = Dygraph(df1, group="weather").range_selector()
>>> b = Dygraph(df2, group="weather").range_selector()

Render to different targets:

>>> html = chart.to_html()        # standalone HTML page
>>> config = chart.to_dict()      # raw Python dict (introspection)
>>> payload = chart.to_js()       # JSON-safe dict (Dash / Shiny)
Notes

Dygraph is intentionally framework-agnostic — same pattern as :class:plotly.graph_objects.Figure and R's htmlwidgets. To render in Dash, wrap with :class:dygraphs.dash.DygraphChart. To render in Shiny, use :func:dygraphs.shiny.dygraph_ui in the UI and :func:dygraphs.shiny.render_dygraph in the server. For standalone rendering (reports, emails, iframes), :meth:to_html returns a complete HTML page.

See Also

options : Set global dygraph options (dyOptions). series : Configure a single series by name (dySeries). axis : Configure an axis (dyAxis). legend : Configure the legend (dyLegend). highlight : Configure hover highlighting (dyHighlight). annotation : Add a single annotation (dyAnnotation). shading : Add a shaded region (dyShading). event : Add a vertical event line (dyEvent). limit : Add a horizontal limit line (dyLimit). range_selector : Add the bottom range-selector widget. roller : Add the rolling-average widget. callbacks : Attach JS event callbacks (dyCallbacks). sync_group : Chainable alias for the constructor group= kwarg. group : Style a list of series together (dyGroup) — do not confuse with the group= constructor kwarg. to_dict : Serialise to a Python dict (raw JS objects retained). to_js : Serialise to a JSON-safe dict with __JS__:code:__JS__ markers (for Dash / Shiny callback returns). to_html : Render to a self-contained HTML page. dygraphs.dash.DygraphChart : Dash component wrapper. dygraphs.shiny.dygraph_ui : Shiny UI helper. dygraphs.shiny.render_dygraph : Shiny server-side render helper.

    connect_separated_points=False,
    stroke_width=1.0,
    stroke_pattern=None,
    stroke_border_width=None,
    stroke_border_color="white",
    plotter=None,
    colors=None,
    color_value=0.5,
    color_saturation=1.0,
    draw_x_axis=True,
    draw_y_axis=True,
    include_zero=False,
    draw_axes_at_zero=False,
    logscale=False,
    axis_tick_size=3.0,
    axis_line_color="black",
    axis_line_width=0.3,
    axis_label_color="black",
    axis_label_font_size=14,
    axis_label_width=60,
    draw_grid=True,
    grid_line_color=None,
    grid_line_width=0.3,
    title_height=None,
    right_gap=5,
    digits_after_decimal=2,
    labels_kmb=False,
    labels_kmg2=False,
    labels_utc=False,
    max_number_width=6,
    sig_figs=None,
    pan_edge_fraction=None,
    animated_zooms=False,
    disable_zoom=False,
    retain_date_window=False,
    error_bars=False,
    custom_bars=False,
    sigma=None,
    fractions=False,
    wilson_interval=True,
    visibility=None,
    legend_formatter=None,
    range_selector_plot_line_width=None,
    range_selector_plot_fill_gradient_color=None,
    range_selector_background_line_width=None,
    range_selector_background_stroke_color=None,
    range_selector_foreground_stroke_color=None,
    range_selector_foreground_line_width=None,
    range_selector_alpha=None,
    grid_line_pattern=None,
    resizable=None,
    pixel_ratio=None,
    stacked_graph_nan_fill=None,
    animate_background_fade=True,
    x_label_height=None,
    y_label_width=None,
    legend_follow_offset_x=None,
    legend_follow_offset_y=None,
    range_selector_veil_colour=None,
    delimiter=None,
    x_value_parser=None,
    display_annotations=False,
    data_handler=None,
    mobile_disable_y_touch=True,
    use_data_timezone=False,
)

Set global chart options.

Mirrors R dyOptions.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION stacked_graph

If set, stack series on top of one another rather than drawing them independently. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

fill_graph

Fill the area underneath the graph. Not compatible with error bars. Can also be set per-series. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

fill_alpha

Transparency for filled regions (0.0 = invisible, 1.0 = as dark as the line). By default 0.15.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 0.15

step_plot

Display as a step plot instead of a line plot. Can also be set per-series. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

stem_plot

Display as a stem plot instead of a line plot. Can also be set per-series. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

draw_points

Draw a small dot at each data point. Can also be set per-series. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

point_size

Size in pixels of the dot drawn at each point. Also controls the size of dots at isolated points. By default 1.0.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 1.0

point_shape

Shape of the dot drawn at each point. One of "dot", "triangle", "square", "diamond", "pentagon", "hexagon", "circle", "star", "plus", or "ex". By default "dot".

TYPE: str DEFAULT: 'dot'

draw_gap_edge_points

Draw points at the edges of gaps in the data. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

connect_separated_points

If True, connect points even when there are missing values in between (useful when different series have data at different x-values). By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

stroke_width

Width in pixels of the lines connecting data points. Can also be set per-series. By default 1.0.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 1.0

stroke_pattern

A predefined pattern ("dotted", "dashed", "dotdash", "solid") or a custom array where even indices are draw lengths and odd are space lengths in pixels. Can also be set per-series. By default None (solid line).

TYPE: str | list[int] | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_border_width

Draw a border around graph lines to make crossing lines more distinguishable. Can also be set per-series. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_border_color

Color for the line border when stroke_border_width is set. Can also be set per-series. By default "white".

TYPE: str DEFAULT: 'white'

plotter

A JavaScript function (or array of functions) which plots each data series. May also be set per-series. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

colors

Colors for the data series, e.g. "#AABBCC", "rgb(255,100,200)", or "yellow". If not specified, equally-spaced points around a colour wheel are used. By default None.

TYPE: list[str] | None DEFAULT: None

color_value

Value component (0.0--1.0) of auto-generated series colours in HSV space. By default 0.5.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 0.5

color_saturation

Saturation component (0.0--1.0) of auto-generated series colours in HSV space. By default 1.0.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 1.0

draw_x_axis

Whether to draw the x-axis. Setting to False also prevents x-axis ticks and reclaims the space. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

draw_y_axis

Whether to draw the y-axis. Setting to False also prevents y-axis ticks and reclaims the space. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

include_zero

Always include zero in the y-axis range, typically as the lowest value. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

draw_axes_at_zero

Draw the X axis at Y=0 and the Y axis at X=0 if those positions are inside the visible area; otherwise draw at the edges as usual. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

logscale

Show the y-axis in log scale. Values <= 0 are not displayed. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

axis_tick_size

Spacing between axis labels and tick marks. By default 3.0.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 3.0

axis_line_color

Color of x- and y-axis lines (CSS string). By default "black".

TYPE: str DEFAULT: 'black'

axis_line_width

Thickness in pixels of x- and y-axis lines. By default 0.3.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 0.3

axis_label_color

Color for x- and y-axis labels (CSS string). By default "black".

TYPE: str DEFAULT: 'black'

axis_label_font_size

Font size in pixels for axis labels. By default 14.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 14

axis_label_width

Width in pixels of the divs containing axis labels. By default 60.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 60

draw_grid

Whether to display grid lines. Can also be set per-axis. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

grid_line_color

Color of grid lines. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

grid_line_width

Thickness in pixels of grid lines. By default 0.3.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 0.3

title_height

Height in pixels of the chart title; also controls its default font size. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

right_gap

Pixels of blank space at the right edge, making it easier to highlight the rightmost point. By default 5.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 5

digits_after_decimal

Number of digits after the decimal point in normal display mode. By default 2.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 2

labels_kmb

Show K/M/B for thousands/millions/billions on y-axis. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

labels_kmg2

Show k/M/G for kilo/Mega/Giga on y-axis (base 2). By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

labels_utc

Show date/time labels in UTC instead of local time. Incompatible with use_data_timezone. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

max_number_width

If a number has more than this many digits left of the decimal, switch to scientific notation. By default 6.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 6

sig_figs

Display a fixed number of significant figures instead of fixed decimal places. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

pan_edge_fraction

Farthest a graph may be panned, as a fraction of the display (e.g. 0.1 = 10%). None means no bounds. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

animated_zooms

Animate the transition between zoom windows. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

disable_zoom

Disable click-and-drag zooming. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

retain_date_window

Keep the current zoom level when updating the chart with new data. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

error_bars

Enable symmetric error bars. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

custom_bars

Enable custom (asymmetric) bars. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

sigma

When error_bars is True, number of standard deviations to shade. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

fractions

If True, values are interpreted as fractions and error bars are drawn using a Wilson confidence interval. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

wilson_interval

Use a Wilson confidence interval when fractions is True. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

visibility

Array of booleans controlling per-series visibility. By default None.

TYPE: list[bool] | None DEFAULT: None

legend_formatter

JavaScript function to format legend values. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

range_selector_plot_line_width

Line width in the range selector mini plot. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

range_selector_plot_fill_gradient_color

Gradient color in the range selector fill. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

range_selector_background_line_width

Background line width in the range selector. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

range_selector_background_stroke_color

Background stroke color in the range selector. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

range_selector_foreground_stroke_color

Foreground stroke color in the range selector. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

range_selector_foreground_line_width

Foreground line width in the range selector. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

range_selector_alpha

Alpha transparency of the range selector. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

grid_line_pattern

Custom dash pattern for grid lines. By default None.

TYPE: list[int] | None DEFAULT: None

resizable

If set (e.g. "both"), allow the chart to be resized by the user. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

pixel_ratio

Custom pixel ratio for high-DPI rendering. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

stacked_graph_nan_fill

How to handle NaN in stacked graphs. By default None.

TYPE: ('all', 'inside', 'none') DEFAULT: "all"

animate_background_fade

Animate the background fade effect. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

x_label_height

Height in pixels of the x-axis label. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

y_label_width

Width in pixels of the y-axis label. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

legend_follow_offset_x

Horizontal pixel offset for the follow legend. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

legend_follow_offset_y

Vertical pixel offset for the follow legend. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

range_selector_veil_colour

Color of the veil drawn over deselected areas of the range selector. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

delimiter

Delimiter for CSV data parsing. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

x_value_parser

JavaScript function to parse x-values from CSV. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

display_annotations

Display annotations from data columns. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

data_handler

Custom JavaScript data handler. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

mobile_disable_y_touch

Disable touch events on the Y axis for mobile devices to avoid interfering with scrolling. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

use_data_timezone

Use the timezone of the underlying data for display. Incompatible with labels_utc. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

RETURNS DESCRIPTION Dygraph

Self, for chaining.

Examples:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).options(
...     fill_graph=True,
...     draw_points=True,
...     colors=["#00d4aa", "#f4a261"],
...     stroke_width=2,
... )

Enable stacked graph with grid styling:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).options(
...     stacked_graph=True,
...     grid_line_color="#ddd",
...     axis_label_font_size=12,
... )
See Also

axis : Per-axis configuration. series : Per-series configuration.

docs.title: axis
docs.text:
axis(
    name,
    *,
    label=None,
    value_range=None,
    logscale=None,
    ticker=None,
    range_pad=None,
    label_width=None,
    label_height=None,
    axis_height=None,
    axis_line_color=None,
    axis_line_width=None,
    pixels_per_label=None,
    axis_label_color=None,
    axis_label_font_size=None,
    axis_label_width=None,
    axis_label_formatter=None,
    value_formatter=None,
    draw_grid=None,
    grid_line_color=None,
    grid_line_width=None,
    independent_ticks=None,
)

Configure a specific axis.

Mirrors R dyAxis. Options that are not set explicitly use the global default from :meth:options.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION name

Axis to configure.

TYPE: ('x', 'y', 'y2') DEFAULT: "x"

label

Label to display for the axis. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

value_range

Explicitly set the range to (low, high). If either limit is None it is calculated automatically. By default None.

TYPE: tuple[float | None, float | None] | None DEFAULT: None

logscale

Show this axis in log scale. Values <= 0 are not displayed. Ignored for date-based x-axes. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

ticker

JavaScript function to generate custom tick marks. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

range_pad

Extra space in pixels around the value range to keep edge points visible. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

label_width

Width in pixels of the div containing the y-axis label (affects the rotated label's height). By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

label_height

Height in pixels of the x-axis label div. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

axis_height

Height in pixels of the x-axis. Only applicable to the x-axis. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

axis_line_color

Color of the axis line (CSS string). By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

axis_line_width

Thickness in pixels of the axis line. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

pixels_per_label

Minimum pixel spacing between axis labels. Larger values yield a sparser axis. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

axis_label_color

Color for axis labels (CSS string). By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

axis_label_font_size

Font size in pixels for axis labels. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

axis_label_width

Width in pixels of axis labels. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

axis_label_formatter

JavaScript function to format tick values displayed on the axis. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

value_formatter

JavaScript function to format values shown on mouse-over. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

draw_grid

Whether to display grid lines for this axis. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

grid_line_color

Color of grid lines for this axis. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

grid_line_width

Thickness in pixels of grid lines. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

independent_ticks

Only valid for y and y2. When True, the axis has its own independent ticks; when False, ticks align to the other y-axis. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

RETURNS DESCRIPTION Dygraph

Self, for chaining.

RAISES DESCRIPTION ValueError

If name is not one of "x", "y", "y2", or if axis_height is specified for a non-x axis.

Examples:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).axis("y", label="Temperature", value_range=(0, 40))

Dual y-axis with independent ticks:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).axis(
...     "y2", label="Humidity", independent_ticks=True
... )
See Also

options : Global chart options.

docs.title: series
docs.text:
series(
    name=None,
    *,
    label=None,
    color=None,
    axis="y",
    step_plot=None,
    stem_plot=None,
    fill_graph=None,
    draw_points=None,
    point_size=None,
    point_shape=None,
    stroke_width=None,
    stroke_pattern=None,
    stroke_border_width=None,
    stroke_border_color=None,
    plotter=None,
    highlight_circle_size=None,
    show_in_range_selector=None,
    columns=None,
)

Configure display options for a data series.

Mirrors R dySeries. Options not set explicitly use the global defaults from :meth:options.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION name

Series name (must match a column label). If None, the first unprocessed series is used. By default None.

R-style error-band shortcut. If a list/tuple of 2 or 3 column names is passed instead of a single string, it is forwarded to columns= — mirrors R's dySeries(c("low","mid","high")).

TYPE: str | list[str] | tuple[str, ...] | None DEFAULT: None

label

Display label for the series. Uses name if not set. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

color

CSS color string (e.g. "#AABBCC", "rgb(255,100,200)", "yellow"). If not specified, the global colour palette is used. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

axis

Y-axis to associate the series with. By default "y".

TYPE: ('y', 'y2') DEFAULT: "y"

step_plot

Display as a step plot instead of a line plot. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

stem_plot

Display as a stem plot instead of a line plot. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

fill_graph

Fill the area underneath the series. Not compatible with error bars. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

draw_points

Draw a small dot at each data point. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

point_size

Size in pixels of the dot drawn at each point. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

point_shape

Shape of the dot (see :meth:options for valid shapes). By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_width

Width in pixels of the line connecting points. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_pattern

A predefined pattern ("dotted", "dashed", "dotdash", "solid") or a custom array of draw/space lengths. By default None.

TYPE: str | list[int] | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_border_width

Border width around the line to make crossing lines more distinguishable. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_border_color

Color for the line border. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

plotter

JavaScript plotter function for this series. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

highlight_circle_size

Size in pixels of the circle drawn over a highlighted point. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

show_in_range_selector

Whether to show this series in the range selector mini plot. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

columns

For error bar series, pass 2 or 3 column names:

  • 2 names [value, error] -- symmetric error bars (sets errorBars=True).
  • 3 names [low, mid, high] -- custom bars (sets customBars=True).

The columns are merged into a single series with tuple values and the consumed columns are removed. By default None.

TYPE: list[str] | None DEFAULT: None

RETURNS DESCRIPTION Dygraph

Self, for chaining.

Examples:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).series("temp", color="red", stroke_width=2)

Step plot with custom point shape:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).series(
...     "temp", step_plot=True, draw_points=True, point_shape="square"
... )

Assign a series to the secondary y-axis:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).series("rain", axis="y2", color="blue")
See Also

group : Configure multiple series at once. options : Global chart options.

docs.title: group
docs.text:
group(
    names,
    *,
    label=None,
    color=None,
    axis="y",
    step_plot=None,
    stem_plot=None,
    fill_graph=None,
    draw_points=None,
    point_size=None,
    point_shape=None,
    stroke_width=None,
    stroke_pattern=None,
    stroke_border_width=None,
    stroke_border_color=None,
    plotter=None,
)

Configure a group of series with shared options.

Mirrors R dyGroup. Options are applied uniformly across all named series. List arguments (color, label, point_shape) are cycled if shorter than names.

Note: dyGroup turns off stackedGraph in R because stacking calculates cumulatives over all series, not just a subset.

.. warning:: Not to be confused with the constructor kwarg group=. The two features share the name but do completely different things:

- **This method (``.group([names], ...)``)** styles a *list of
  series* together within a single chart. Takes a list of
  column names. Mirrors R's ``dyGroup()``.
- **Constructor kwarg ``Dygraph(df, group="...")``** (a.k.a.
  :meth:`sync_group`) synchronises zoom, pan, and highlight
  across *multiple charts* that share the same group name.
  Takes a string. Mirrors R's ``dygraph(group=)``.
PARAMETER DESCRIPTION names

Series names (must match column labels).

TYPE: list[str]

label

Display labels for each series. By default None.

TYPE: list[str] | None DEFAULT: None

color

Colors for each series (CSS strings). By default None.

TYPE: list[str] | None DEFAULT: None

axis

Y-axis for all series in the group. By default "y".

TYPE: ('y', 'y2') DEFAULT: "y"

step_plot

Display as step plots. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

stem_plot

Display as stem plots. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

fill_graph

Fill the area underneath each series. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

draw_points

Draw a dot at each point. By default None.

TYPE: bool | None DEFAULT: None

point_size

Size in pixels of each dot. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

point_shape

Shapes for each series' dots. By default None.

TYPE: list[str] | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_width

Line width in pixels. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_pattern

Stroke pattern (see :meth:options). By default None.

TYPE: str | list[int] | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_border_width

Border width around lines. By default None.

TYPE: float | None DEFAULT: None

stroke_border_color

Border color for lines. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

plotter

JavaScript plotter function for the group (used for grouped custom plotters like multi-column). By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

RETURNS DESCRIPTION Dygraph

Self, for chaining.

Examples:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).group(
...     ["temp", "rain"], color=["red", "blue"], draw_points=True
... )
See Also

series : Configure a single series. sync_group : Set the cross-chart sync name (the unrelated group= kwarg).

docs.title: legend
docs.text:
legend(
    show="auto",
    width=250,
    show_zero_values=True,
    labels_div=None,
    labels_separate_lines=False,
    hide_on_mouse_out=True,
)

Configure the series legend.

Mirrors R dyLegend.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION show

When to display the legend. "auto" shows "always" when more than one series is plotted, "onmouseover" for a single series. "follow" shows the legend as an overlay that follows the mouse. By default "auto".

TYPE: ('auto', 'always', 'onmouseover', 'follow', 'never') DEFAULT: "auto"

width

Width in pixels of the legend div. By default 250.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 250

show_zero_values

Show zero-value labels in the legend. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

labels_div

ID of an external div to display data labels in, rather than on the graph. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

labels_separate_lines

Put a line break between series labels. Often used with labels_div. By default False.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

hide_on_mouse_out

Hide the legend when the mouse leaves the chart area. Also affects :meth:highlight hiding. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

RETURNS DESCRIPTION Dygraph

Self, for chaining.

Examples:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).legend(show="always")

Floating legend that follows the mouse:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).legend(show="follow", width=200)
See Also

highlight : Mouse-over highlighting behaviour.

docs.title: highlight
docs.text:
highlight(
    circle_size=3,
    series_background_alpha=0.5,
    series_background_color=None,
    series_opts=None,
    hide_on_mouse_out=True,
)

Configure series mouse-over highlighting.

Mirrors R dyHighlight. Highlighting is always enabled; this method customises its appearance.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION circle_size

Size in pixels of the dot drawn over highlighted points. By default 3.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 3

series_background_alpha

Fade the background while highlighting a series. 1.0 = fully visible background (disable fading), 0.0 = hidden background (show highlighted series only). By default 0.5.

TYPE: float DEFAULT: 0.5

series_background_color

Background color used when fading non-highlighted series. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

series_opts

Options applied to the series closest to the mouse pointer (e.g. {"strokeWidth": 3}). See :meth:series for valid keys. By default None.

TYPE: dict[str, Any] | None DEFAULT: None

hide_on_mouse_out

Hide highlighting effects when the mouse leaves the chart area. Also affects :meth:legend hiding. By default True.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

RETURNS DESCRIPTION Dygraph

Self, for chaining.

Examples:

>>> chart = Dygraph(df).highlight(
...     circle_size=5,
...     series_background_alpha=0.3,
...     series_opts={"strokeWidth": 3},
... )
See Also

legend : Legend display options.

docs.text:
annotation(
    x,
    text,
    *,
    tooltip=None,
    width=None,
    height=None,
    css_class=None,
    tick_height=None,
    tick_color=None,
    tick_width=None,
    attach_at_bottom=False,
    icon=None,
    click_handler=None,
    mouse_over_handler=None,
    mouse_out_handler=None,
    dbl_click_handler=None,
    series=None,
)

Add a text annotation at a data point.

Mirrors R dyAnnotation. Annotations are bound to a specific series; if not specified, the last series is used.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION x

Position on the x-axis (numeric or date/time value).

TYPE: Any

text

Short text to overlay on the chart at x.

TYPE: str

tooltip

Additional text displayed on mouse hover. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

width

Width in pixels of the annotation flag. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

height

Height in pixels of the annotation flag. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

css_class

CSS class for styling the annotation. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

tick_height

Height in pixels of the tick connecting the point to its flag or icon. By default None.

TYPE: int | None DEFAULT: None

tick_color

Color of the tick mark. By default None.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

to_dict()

Serialise the full dygraph config to a plain dict.

This is the JSON payload that gets sent to the browser, equivalent to the x list in R's htmlwidgets::createWidget.

Contains raw JS(code) objects for things like the default interaction model — safe for :meth:to_html (inlines into a <script> block), NOT safe for Dash callback returns (Dash's prop validator only accepts dash components, strings, dicts, numbers, None, and lists of those). Use :meth:to_js for Dash callbacks — it substitutes "__JS__:code:__JS__" string markers that the clientside renderer evaluates.

Examples:

>>> config = Dygraph(df).options(fill_graph=True).to_dict()
>>> config["attrs"]["fillGraph"]
True

docs.title: Dash Integration
docs.text:
Bases: ComponentWrapper

Dash component for a dygraphs chart.

Usage mirrors dcc.Graph::

from dygraphs import Dygraph
from dygraphs.dash import DygraphChart

app.layout = html.Div([
    DygraphChart(figure=dg, id="chart"),
])

What it actually is. A DygraphChart is an html.Div that contains two siblings:

  • dcc.Store(id=id, data=<serialised config>) — the chart store. The chart's identity in the callback registry. Its id equals the user-supplied id.
  • html.Div(id=f"{id}-container") — the container where dygraphs renders its JS-driven DOM.

The class inherits from :class:dash_wrap.ComponentWrapper with the Store as the inner component, so the callback registry resolves Output(chart, ...) to the Store's id. Two small pieces of machinery make that work — both shipped by dash-wrap, both documented in its README, no magic beyond that:

  • _set_random_id is overridden on the wrapper to return the inner Store's id. Dash's dependency wiring calls this when it sees a component passed to Output / Input / State.
  • __class__ is a property that returns the inner Store's class, so isinstance(chart, dcc.Store) is True. The C-level type(chart) is still DygraphChart, which is what Dash's serialiser uses to emit the DOM (a normal <div>).

A per-instance clientside callback is registered at construction. It listens to the store and dispatches to window.dygraphsDash.render(setup, config). The callback body inlines dash_render.js (IIFE-guarded, idempotent) and a tiny per-chart setup payload built by :func:_build_render_js.

Any change to chart options — stroke width, colors, legend — is pushed through the same primary store as a fresh config (full destroy+recreate, matching R's renderDygraph model). There's no separate "options override" channel; one write path, one mental model. See CLAUDE.md "Decisions deferred / opts store" if you hit a performance wall and want to reintroduce a cheaper cosmetic-updates channel.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION figure

Configured Dygraph builder instance, or None for an empty placeholder. With None the primary store holds data=None and the clientside renderer early-returns (if (!config) return;); users push the first real config via Output(chart, "data"). Use case: building an app layout where the first chart state arrives only after a callback fires (e.g. a button click).

TYPE: Dygraph | None

id

User-facing DOM id. Assigned to the primary dcc.Store; the opts store gets {id}-opts and the container div {id}-container. Auto-generated if omitted.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

height

Chart height in pixels or a CSS height string.

TYPE: str | int DEFAULT: '400px'

width

CSS width for the container div.

TYPE: str DEFAULT: '100%'

modebar

Show the Plotly-style hover overlay (capture + reset-zoom).

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

Examples:

Updating the chart's data — including restyles — via to_js, which returns a Dash-safe dict (see :meth:Dygraph.to_js)::

@callback(Output(chart, "data"), Input("btn", "n_clicks"))
def refresh(n):
    return Dygraph(df * n).to_js()

@callback(Output(chart, "data"), Input("thick", "n_clicks"))
def thick(n):
    return (
        Dygraph(df)
        .options(stroke_width=3 if n else 1)
        .to_js()
    )

Bases: ComponentWrapper

Canvas-based stacked bar chart with interactive range selector.

Sibling of :class:DygraphChart — same class-based construction pattern, same dash-wrap identity mechanics — but a completely different renderer: this one draws directly on a <canvas> and does not use dygraphs.js at all. The trade-off is interactivity scope: built-in pan / zoom via a range selector, but no group sync with highlights the way :class:DygraphChart has.

Layout — two siblings inside an html.Div:

  • dcc.Store(id={id}, data=initial_data_csv) — the chart's identity. Push a fresh CSV string via Output(chart, "data").
  • html.Div(id={id}-container) — the container where the canvas is drawn.
PARAMETER DESCRIPTION id

User-facing DOM id. Assigned to the data store; container gets {id}-container. Auto-generated if omitted.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

initial_data

Initial CSV data. Must start with a Date,... header row. Empty string renders nothing until a callback pushes data.

TYPE: str DEFAULT: ''

colors

Hex / CSS colors, one per non-date column. Falls back to a built-in palette when omitted.

TYPE: list[str] | None DEFAULT: None

height

Main chart height in pixels.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 300

title

Chart title, drawn on the canvas above the plot.

TYPE: str DEFAULT: ''

selector_height

Height of the range-selector strip at the bottom.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 40

group

Sync group name. Charts sharing the same name coordinate their x-axis window via the window.__dyGroups registry — same mechanism used by :class:DygraphChart.

TYPE: str | None DEFAULT: None

Examples:

::

from dygraphs.dash import StackedBarChart

chart = StackedBarChart(
    id="bars",
    initial_data="Date,A,B\n2024-01-01,1,2\n2024-01-02,3,4",
    title="Stacked demo",
)

@callback(Output(chart, "data"), Input("refresh", "n_clicks"))
def refresh(_n):
    return updated_csv_string

docs.title: chart_id property
docs.text:
chart_id

The chart id — equals the inner dcc.Store's id.

Exposed under chart_id rather than id because Dash's layout validation reads .id on every component to detect duplicates. Proxying id to the inner store would make the outer wrapper and the inner store look like duplicates even though only the inner is rendered with an id.

Use this when you need the id as a string — for example to construct sibling ids (f"{chart.chart_id}-download") or pass to :class:dash.Output alongside derived ids. For the direct callback wire-up, Output(chart, "data") works just as well (dash-wrap resolves it to the store's id).

docs.title: __getattr__
docs.text:
__getattr__(name)

Proxy to dash-wrap's default handler, with one friendly error.

Users coming from dcc.Graph habitually reach for chart.id. The wrapper deliberately has no id attribute (to avoid Dash's duplicate-id validation tripping against the inner store), so the default lookup would raise a generic AttributeError. Intercept that one case with a message that points to :attr:chart_id.

docs.title: Shiny Integration
docs.text:
Create the UI components for a dygraph chart.

Returns a TagList containing:

  • CDN <link> and <script> for dygraphs
  • The shared render_core.js asset (idempotent; only the first inline registers window.dygraphs)
  • A container <div> Shiny will target via custom message
  • A <script> registering the custom message handler for this specific element_id, which dispatches into window.dygraphs.render(container, config)
PARAMETER DESCRIPTION element_id

Unique DOM id for the chart container.

TYPE: str

height

CSS dimensions.

TYPE: str DEFAULT: '400px'

width

CSS dimensions.

TYPE: str DEFAULT: '400px'

RETURNS DESCRIPTION shiny.ui.TagList

Send dygraph config to the browser via Shiny custom message.

Call this from a reactive effect or observer to render / update the chart. Passing dg=None clears the chart (matches the empty- placeholder semantics of :class:dygraphs.dash.DygraphChart constructed with figure=None) — the clientside renderer early-returns on falsy config.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION session

Shiny session object.

TYPE: Shiny session

element_id

DOM id matching the dygraph_ui() call.

TYPE: str

dg

Configured Dygraph builder instance, or None to clear.

TYPE: Dygraph | None

docs.title: Capture
docs.text:
Create a capture strategy compatible with dash_capture.capture_element().

The returned strategy composites every visible <canvas> inside the target element onto a single white-backed canvas and overlays the HTML layer — chart title, x/y axis labels, tick labels, legend, annotations — via html2canvas, returning a base64 data-URI. Designed for charts produced by :class:dygraphs.dash.DygraphChart.

The capture JS is :data:MULTI_CANVAS_CAPTURE_JS — the same code invoked by the chart modebar's camera-icon download — so the two paths cannot drift apart.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION hide_range_selector

If True, temporarily hide the range-selector canvases (.dygraph-rangesel-fgcanvas, .dygraph-rangesel-bgcanvas, .dygraph-rangesel-zoomhandle) before capture and restore their original display values afterwards.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: True

format

Output image format. The format is baked into the JS at strategy build time, so the wizard's runtime fmt argument is ignored — build a separate strategy per format if you need a chooser.

TYPE: ('png', 'jpeg', 'webp') DEFAULT: "png"

debug

If True, the capture JS logs devicePixelRatio, target element dimensions, output canvas dimensions, the bounding rect of every source canvas, and the html2canvas overlay dimensions to the browser console; it also draws a 1px red border around each source canvas's destination rect in the output. Use this to diagnose cropping or overlay-alignment issues.

TYPE: bool DEFAULT: False

settle_frames

When _params declares capture_width / capture_height, the strategy live-resizes the chart container so dygraphs's ResizeObserver (in :file:render_core.js) redraws the canvases at the target dimensions. settle_frames is the number of requestAnimationFrame ticks awaited between the resize and the capture. Bump this if you see partial redraws.

TYPE: int DEFAULT: 2

_params

Internal hook mirroring :func:dash_capture.html2canvas_strategy. Pass inspect.signature(renderer).parameters to enable target-size capture: when the renderer declares capture_width and/or capture_height, this strategy emits a live-resize preprocess that runs before the multi-canvas merge.

TYPE: Mapping or None DEFAULT: None

RETURNS DESCRIPTION CaptureStrategy

A dash_capture.strategies.CaptureStrategy instance, ready to pass to capture_element(..., strategy=...).

RAISES DESCRIPTION ImportError

If dash-capture is not installed. Install with pip install dygraphs[dash] dash-capture.

Notes

SVG output is not supported: dygraphs renders to <canvas>, and canvas.toDataURL only emits raster formats.

The HTML overlay rendered by html2canvas is rasterised at the same devicePixelRatio as the canvas composite, so text stays crisp on retina displays. Compositing order is canvases first, overlays on top — so labels never get drawn over by axis lines.

The html2canvas asset is queued via :func:dash_capture._html2canvas.ensure_html2canvas at strategy construction time. Call dygraph_strategy() at module level (or anywhere before the first page is served) so Dash can drain the inline-script queue into the served HTML.

Examples:

Wire a capture button next to a chart::

from dash import Dash, html
from dash_capture import capture_element

from dygraphs import Dygraph
from dygraphs.dash import DygraphChart, dygraph_strategy

app = Dash(__name__)

chart = Dygraph(df, title="Sales").range_selector()
chart_component = DygraphChart(figure=chart, id="sales", height="320px")

app.layout = html.Div([
    chart_component,
    capture_element(
        "sales-container",
        trigger="Download PNG",
        strategy=dygraph_strategy(hide_range_selector=True),
        filename="sales.png",
    ),
])

The element id passed to capture_element is f"{id}-container" — the inner <div> that holds the chart canvases (see :class:dygraphs.dash.DygraphChart).

docs.title: Utilities
docs.text:
Wrapper to mark a string as raw JavaScript.

Mirrors R htmlwidgets::JS. When serialised to JSON the string is emitted verbatim (without quotes) so that the browser can evaluate it as code.

PARAMETER DESCRIPTION code

Raw JavaScript source code.

TYPE: str

Examples:

>>> js = JS("function(x) { return x * 2; }")
>>> repr(js)
"JS('function(x) { return x * 2; }')"
